perturbation.original_text: The Board Member agrees that the Confidential Information he or she receives shall be used solely for the benefit of the HNBA Board Work, and that all rights to the proprietary and novel features contained in the Confidential Information are reserved by the disclosing Party. The Board Member will not use or disclose Confidential Information to any third party for any purpose other than the performance of his or her duties as a Board Member of the HNBA unless and until the HNBA expressly authorizes the disclosure in writing. Board Member specifically agrees not to use any personally identifiable information provided by or on behalf of the HNBA, its contractors, affiliates, vendors, sponsors or employees, for any direct marketing and not to transfer such information to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: The Board Member agrees that the Confidential Information he or she receives shall be used primarily for the benefit of the HNBA Board Work, and that all rights to the proprietary and novel features contained in the Confidential Information are generally reserved by the disclosing Party. The Board Member will endeavor not to use or disclose Confidential Information to any third party for purposes other than the performance of his or her duties as a Board Member of the HNBA unless the HNBA authorizes the disclosure. Board Member agrees not to knowingly use any personally identifiable information provided by or on behalf of the HNBA, its contractors, affiliates, vendors, sponsors or employees, for any direct marketing and will try to avoid transferring such information to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: The modifications weaken the language of the agreement. Replacing 'solely' with 'primarily,' 'reserved' with 'generally reserved,' 'will not use or disclose' with 'endeavor not to use or disclose,' 'expressly authorizes' with 'authorizes', 'agrees not to' with 'agrees not to knowingly', and 'not to transfer' with 'will try to avoid transferring' all introduce ambiguity and reduce the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations. This weakens the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Official Code § 32-509 restricts the use of personal information and requires explicit consent. The modified text, by using terms like "primarily" and "endeavor", introduces ambiguity about the permissible uses of confidential information, especially personal data. It weakens the obligation to protect this data and does not ensure the individual's rights are protected according to the D.C. law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 32â509. Administrative enforcement procedure; relief. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 32â509. Administrative enforcement procedure; relief. (a) The Mayor shall provide an administrative procedure pursuant to which a person claimed to be aggrieved under this chapter may file a complaint against an employer alleged to have violated this chapter. A complaint shall be filed within 1 year of the occurrence or discovery of the alleged violation of this chapter. (b) The administrative procedure shall include, but not be limited to: (1) An investigation of the complaint and an attempt to resolve the complaint by conference, conciliation, or persuasion; (2) If the complaint is not resolved, a determination on the existence of probable cause to believe a violation of this chapter has occurred; (3) If there is a determination that probable cause exists, the issuance and service of a written notice and a copy of the complaint to the employer alleged to have committed the violation that requires the employer to answer the charges of the complaint at a formal hearing; (4) A hearing conducted in accordance with procedures that the Mayor shall promulgate pursuant to subchapter I of Chapter 5 of Title 2 ; (5) A decision and order accompanied by findings of fact and conclusions of law; (6) If there is a determination that an employer committed a violation of this chapter, the issuance of an order that requires the employer to pay the employee damages in an amount equal to: (A) Any wages, salary, employment benefits, or other compensation denied or lost to the employee due to the violation plus interest on the amount calculated at the rate prescribed in Â§ 28-3302(b) or (c); and (B) An amount equal to the greater of: (i) The amount determined under subparagraph (A) of this paragraph; or (ii) Consequential damages not to exceed an amount equal to 3 times the amount determined under subparagraph (A) of this paragraph plus any medical expenses not
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguities that weaken the protection of confidential information, specifically personal data. The original text uses definitive language, whereas the revised text uses terms like "primarily," "endeavor not to," and "agrees not to knowingly," which create uncertainty regarding the permissible uses and protection of confidential information. While D.C. Official Code § 32-509 isn't explicitly quoted in the snippet provided, the law explanation states it restricts the use of personal information and requires explicit consent. The modifications could potentially lead to non-compliance with requirements for protecting personal data, creating a legal contradiction as defined by the 'Ambiguities - Ambiguous Legal Obligation' type.

perturbation.original_text: The HNBA agrees that the Board Member shall not be liable for any disclosure or use of any Confidential Information if: (d) The Board Member was legally required to disclose the Confidential Information, provided that the Board Member (i) provides the HNBA and/or other disclosing party with written notice within five (5) days of knowing of such legal requirement so that the HNBA has the opportunity to pursue its rights regarding such potential disclosure, and (ii) utilizes reasonable efforts to obtain reliable assurances that the person receiving such Confidential Information will maintain the confidentiality of the same.
perturbation.changed_text: The HNBA agrees that the Board Member is generally not liable for disclosure or use of any Confidential Information if: (d) The Board Member believes they are legally required to disclose the Confidential Information, provided that the Board Member (i) attempts to provide the HNBA and/or other disclosing party with notice when practical of knowing of such legal requirement so that the HNBA has the opportunity to pursue its rights regarding such potential disclosure, and (ii) considers whether to obtain assurances that the person receiving such Confidential Information will maintain the confidentiality of the same.
perturbation.explanation: The original text provides a specific exception with conditions for legally required disclosures. The modified text uses weaker and more subjective language: 'generally not liable' instead of 'shall not be liable,' 'believes they are legally required' instead of 'was legally required,' 'attempts to provide...when practical' instead of 'provides...within five (5) days,' and 'considers whether to obtain assurances' instead of 'utilizes reasonable efforts to obtain reliable assurances'. This ambiguity makes the exception much broader and weakens the HNBA's ability to protect its confidential information, especially if the 'belief' or 'practicality' are not reasonably assessed.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Municipal Regulations Title 17 § 3103.1 pertains to the confidentiality of information submitted to the Department of Energy and Environment. Though it's focused on environmental data, the principle of requiring strict procedures for legally mandated disclosures to maintain confidentiality is analogous. The change weakens the obligation of the Board Member to protect confidential information, potentially leading to breaches without adequate safeguards, which is contrary to the spirit of maintaining confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The original text strictly defines the conditions under which a Board Member is not liable for disclosing confidential information when legally required. The changed text weakens these conditions by introducing subjective elements and ambiguities. Specifically, changing 'was legally required' to 'believes they are legally required' introduces a subjective standard that could be easily abused. Furthermore, altering 'provides...within five (5) days' to 'attempts to provide...when practical' and 'utilizes reasonable efforts to obtain reliable assurances' to 'considers whether to obtain assurances' significantly reduces the board member's responsibility to protect confidential information. While the scraped snippets don't directly address the specific scenario, the Law Explanation mentions that the change weakens the obligation of the Board Member to protect confidential information, potentially leading to breaches without adequate safeguards, which is contrary to the spirit of maintaining confidentiality. The modified text creates ambiguity and undermines the HNBA's ability to protect its confidential information, leading to a clear legal contradiction under the 'Ambiguities Legal Contradiction' definition, because it creates an ambiguous legal obligation that would be difficult to enforce under state or national law.

perturbation.original_text: Once the purpose of the disclosure is achieved, the HNBA will instruct the Board Member to destroy all Confidential Information supplied to the Board Member by the HNBA.
perturbation.changed_text: Once the primary purpose of the disclosure is generally achieved, the HNBA may suggest that the Board Member consider disposing of Confidential Information supplied to the Board Member by the HNBA.
perturbation.explanation: The original text creates a clear obligation for the HNBA to instruct destruction and for the Board Member to destroy confidential information. The modified text weakens this obligation considerably by changing 'Once the purpose of the disclosure is achieved' to 'Once the primary purpose of the disclosure is generally achieved', 'the HNBA will instruct' to 'the HNBA may suggest', and 'destroy all' to 'consider disposing of'. The ambiguity of 'primary purpose' and the discretionary nature of the suggestion weakens the commitment to data destruction.
perturbation.law_explanation: D.C. Official Code § 28-3851 mandates notification of security breaches involving personal information. Retaining confidential information longer than necessary increases the risk of a data breach. By weakening the obligation to destroy data promptly, the modified text increases the risk of a security breach and potentially violates the spirit, if not the letter, of the law regarding data security.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Â§ 28â3851. Definitions. | D.C. Law Library Â Toggle mobile menu Code of the District of Columbia Â§ 28â3851. Definitions. For purposes of this subchapter, the term: (1) (A) "Breach of the security of the system" means unauthorized acquisition of computerized or other electronic data or any equipment or device storing such data that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information maintained by the person or entity who conducts business in the District of Columbia. (B) The term "breach of the security of the system" does not include: (i) A good-faith acquisition of personal information by an employee or agency of the person or entity for the purposes of the person or entity if the personal information is not used improperly or subject to further unauthorized disclosure; (ii) Acquisition of data that has been rendered secure, including through encryption or redaction of such data, so as to be unusable by an unauthorized third party unless any information obtained has the potential to compromise the effectiveness of the security protection preventing unauthorized access; or (iii) Acquisition of personal information of an individual that the person or entity reasonably determines, after a reasonable investigation and consultation with the Office of the Attorney General for the District of Columbia and federal law enforcement agencies, will likely not result in harm to the individual. (1A) "Genetic information" has the meaning ascribed to it under the federal Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act of 1996 ("HIPAA"), approved August 21, 1996 (Pub. Law 104-191; 110 Stat. 1936), as specified in 45 C.F.R. Â§ 106.103. (1B) "Medical Information" means any information about a consumer's dental, medical, or mental health treatment or diagnosis by a health-care professional. perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the obligation to destroy confidential information, potentially increasing the risk of a data breach, which could contradict the spirit of D.C. Official Code § 28-3851, even if it doesn't directly violate the letter of the law. The original text had a clear directive, while the changed version introduces ambiguity and discretion. The ambiguity creates a contradiction because the 'primary purpose' is subjective, making the obligation unenforceable. Furthermore, snippet 1 defines a breach as 'unauthorized acquisition of computerized or other electronic data...that compromises the security, confidentiality, or integrity of personal information'. By weakening the data destruction obligation, the risk of such a breach increases.
